Fourier Synthesis:
Building an Image from Frequencies

Any image f(x,y) can be decomposed into sinusoidal basis functions. Each point (u,v) in the frequency plane corresponds to one 2D cosine wave:

f(x,y) = ∑u,v Au,v cos[2π(u·x + v·y)]

In radio astronomy each antenna baseline measures exactly one Fourier component: V(u,v) = ∬ I(x,y) · e−2πi(ux+vy) dx dy. Section 1 opens with the Fourier winding intuition. Section 2 shows familiar 1D signals. Section 3 builds a pure 2D cosine sum from scratch. Section 4 reconstructs a portrait from Fourier coefficients. Section 5 shows how adding antennas fills UV coverage.

1 — Fourier Transform: Winding a Signal onto the Complex Plane

The Fourier transform "winds" the signal around the complex plane at ν turns per unit time: g(ν) = ∫ f(t) e−2πiνt dt. The red arrow is the centroid of the wound-up curve — it is large only when f(t) oscillates at frequency ν, causing the winding to bunch up on one side of the plane.

g(ν) = ∫ f(t) · e−2πi ν t dt  — centroid of the winding at speed ν

Signal f(t)

Color = time · red dashes = winding period 1/ν

f(t) · e−2πiνt on the complex plane

Color = time · red arrow = centroid g(ν) · scroll=zoom · drag=rotate · shift+drag=pan · dblclick=reset

Magnitude spectrum |g(ν)|

Blue curve · red marker = current ν
T₀ = 1.00
ν = 1.00

2 — One-Dimensional Fourier Decomposition

Start with a familiar 1D pattern, then rebuild it from low to high harmonics. The partial sum always adds the first few cosine-phase components in order of harmonic number, so the reconstruction shows how sharp edges, trends, and recurring wiggles are encoded across Fourier modes.

Time / Frequency View

Time domain — target, partial sum, and latest harmonic

Scroll to zoom. Drag to rotate. Shift + drag to pan.

Move the harmonic slider or press Play to reveal the first components.

3 — Sum of Cosines

Drag the red dot to choose (u,v), then click Add This Baseline to accumulate cos 2π(ux + vy) into f(x,y). Each dot marks one added baseline.

0 baselines — flat gray

cos 2π(ux + vy) — red dot

Each dot = one cosine baseline

4 — Fourier Synthesis of an Image

The target is the Mandrill portrait. Dot opacity encodes the Fourier amplitude at each (u,v). Auto-add accumulates modes from highest to lowest amplitude — watch the image emerge.

5 — Adding Antennas: How UV Coverage Grows

Each pair of antennas samples one spatial frequency. With N antennas you get N(N−1)/2 baselines — and twice as many UV points when conjugate symmetry is included. Drag the slider to add antennas and watch the frequency-plane coverage fill in.

Array Layout (East–North, km)

2 antennas — 1 baseline

UV Coverage — (u,v) in kλ

2 UV points (± conjugate)
2
37°
1 baseline 2 UV pts

Array: 8-antenna Y-configuration (scaled). UV computed at HA = 0, lat = 37°N, reference wavelength λ = 10 cm (3 GHz). Each color = one antenna added.
